summary:
The Hijra (622 AD) — Muhammad's migration from Mecca to Medina — was the pivotal event in early Islamic history, marking the transition of Islam from a persecuted minority movement to a politically organized community with the capacity for military action and self-governance. Facing increasing persecution in Mecca and a plot on his life, [[event:2124|Muhammad]] and his followers migrated 320 kilometers north to Yathrib (renamed Medina, 'City of the Prophet'), where he had been invited to serve as an arbiter between rival Arab clans. The year of the Hijra (622 AD) became Year 1 of the Islamic calendar — the foundation of Islamic chronology.

In Medina, Muhammad transformed from a preacher into a statesman. The Constitution of Medina — one of the earliest written constitutional documents — established a political community encompassing Muslim, Jewish, and pagan Arab tribes under Muhammad's leadership and set out their mutual rights and obligations. Muhammad conducted military campaigns against Mecca (the Battle of Badr in 624, Uhud in 625, the Trench in 627), negotiated diplomatic agreements, and eventually returned to conquer Mecca in 630 AD with an army of 10,000. The Hijra thus marks the moment when Islam became a civilization-building movement as well as a religion — the prototype of the Muslim community (umma) that would go on to build an empire. The concept of hijra (migration for the sake of one's faith) became a recurring motif in Islamic history.
